sections.text: Welcome back to AP Psychology. Module Twelve — testing and intelligence. This is the unit where psychology tries to put a number on something we don't even fully agree exists. By the end of today you'll know what makes a test actually good, what intelligence even is, why the average IQ score keeps creeping up, and the single biggest trap on the AP exam — confusing reliability with validity. Let's get into it.

Quick thought experiment. Your bathroom scale weighs you four times in a row and gives you four totally different numbers — one fifty, one sixty-eight, one forty-two, one fifty-five. Would you trust it? Of course not. That's a reliability problem. Now imagine a different scale. It says one fifty every single time you step on it. Same number, every time. Reliable. But — what if it's actually weighing your shoes, not you? Or what if it's permanently stuck on one fifty no matter who steps on? Now it's reliable but it isn't measuring what it claims to measure. That's a validity problem. Reliability and validity are NOT the same thing. A broken thermometer that always says seventy-two degrees, even inside a freezer, is perfectly reliable — and completely invalid. Lock that in. AP loves this.

Three things today. One — what makes a test good: reliability, validity, and standardization. Two — what intelligence even is: Spearman, Gardner, Sternberg. Three — IQ tests, the bell curve, the Flynn effect, and the bias controversies that come with all of it. By the end you should be able to read a test description and tell me whether it's reliable, valid, both, or neither.

A good psychological test has to clear three bars. Bar one — reliability. Does the test give consistent results? Same person, same conditions, similar score. Bar two — validity. Does the test actually measure what it claims to measure? An IQ test should measure cognitive ability, not just reading speed. Bar three — standardization. Was the test given the same way to everyone, and was it normed against a comparison group, so we know what an average score actually looks like? You need all three. A test that's reliable but not valid is a broken thermometer. A test that's valid but not reliable is a useful idea you can't actually use. A test that isn't standardized is not really a test — it's an opinion.

Now the subtypes. Reliability comes in three flavors. Test-retest — give the same test twice, weeks apart, and check if the same person scores about the same. Split-half — split the test items in half, score each half separately, and check if the two halves agree. Inter-rater — when scoring is subjective, do two graders give the same score? Validity also comes in three flavors. Content validity — does the test cover the actual material it's supposed to? A driving test that never asks about stop signs has poor content validity. Construct validity — does the test measure the abstract idea, the construct, that it claims? Like, does an anxiety scale really capture anxiety? Criterion validity — and the special case, predictive validity — does the test predict a real-world outcome? The SAT claims to predict college GPA. If it does, that's predictive validity. If it doesn't, the claim is dead. Three reliabilities, three validities. AP will quiz you on which is which.

So what IS intelligence? Charles Spearman in nineteen oh-four noticed something weird. People who scored well on one mental test tended to score well on lots of other mental tests. Reading, math, vocabulary, spatial puzzles — performance on one predicted performance on the others. He called the underlying ability g — for general intelligence. The g factor. The idea is that there's a kind of all-purpose mental horsepower that fuels lots of different skills. Modern researchers still find g pretty hard to argue with statistically. But other psychologists pushed back — they said one number can't possibly capture everything we mean by smart. That argument led to the next two theories.

Two big alternatives to one-number intelligence. First — Howard Gardner's multiple intelligences. Gardner proposed eight separate intelligences, not one. Linguistic — words, writers, lawyers. Logical-mathematical — numbers and logic. Spatial — architects and pilots. Musical — composers and DJs. Bodily-kinesthetic — athletes and dancers. Interpersonal — reading other people. Intrapersonal — knowing yourself. Naturalist — recognizing patterns in nature. Eight kinds of smart. Hugely popular in schools — but here's the AP catch — Gardner's theory has weak empirical support. There's not great evidence that those eight are actually independent. Second — Robert Sternberg's triarchic theory. He argues for three intelligences. Analytical — book smart, the kind schools test. Creative — generating new ideas. Practical — street smart, navigating real-world situations. Sternberg's three are easier to defend than Gardner's eight, but harder to test on a Scantron than Spearman's one. So — Spearman one, Gardner eight, Sternberg three. Three answers to: what counts as smart?

Your turn. A psychologist gives the same students the SAT in October. Same students take the SAT again in March. Their scores are nearly identical. Question one — is this evidence of reliability or validity? Question two — does this prove the SAT predicts college GPA? Pause. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. Question one — same test, same students, two times, same scores. That is test-RETEST reliability. The test is consistent. Question two — no, it does NOT prove the SAT predicts college GPA. That would be predictive validity, and it's a totally separate question. To check that, you'd have to wait, watch the same students get to college, and see if their freshman GPA actually matches their SAT prediction. This is THE trap. Reliability does not prove validity. A broken thermometer that always reads seventy-two degrees is perfectly reliable — same number every time — but it tells you nothing about the actual temperature. A test can be perfectly reliable and still measure the wrong thing. Reliability is necessary but not sufficient. Lock it in.

The classic IQ tests. The original Stanford-Binet, adapted from a French test by Alfred Binet, was Americanized at Stanford by Lewis Terman in nineteen sixteen. Today the dominant tests are David Wechsler's — the WAIS for adults and the WISC for children, school-aged. Both have been scored to a population mean of one hundred and a standard deviation of fifteen. That standard deviation matters. IQ scores form a normal distribution — a bell curve. Sixty-eight percent of people fall within one standard deviation of the mean — that's between eighty-five and one fifteen. Ninety-five percent fall within two standard deviations — between seventy and one thirty. So a score of one thirty is in the top two and a half percent. A score of seventy is in the bottom two and a half percent. AP wants you fluent with the bell curve. Mean one hundred. SD fifteen. Sixty-eight, ninety-five, ninety-nine. Memorize.

Now an oddity. The Flynn effect, named after researcher James Flynn. Across the twentieth century, average IQ scores in industrialized countries have risen — by roughly three points per decade. People taking the same IQ test today, on average, score significantly higher than people who took it in nineteen forty. That's why the tests get re-normed every generation — to keep the mean at a hundred. Why is this happening? Best guesses — better nutrition, more years of formal schooling, smaller families with more individual attention, and a culture saturated with abstract symbols and tests, which trains the kind of thinking IQ tests measure. The Flynn effect doesn't mean humans are getting biologically smarter. It means we're getting better at the specific skills these tests measure. Cause for both optimism and skepticism.

The hard part. Cultural bias and stereotype threat. Cultural bias means a test's content can favor people from one culture or one socioeconomic background. A vocabulary question that uses the word 'regatta' assumes you grew up around sailboats. That's not measuring intelligence — that's measuring exposure. Test designers work hard to strip this out, with mixed success. Then there's stereotype threat. Claude Steele and Joshua Aronson, nineteen ninety-five. Black college students at Stanford, all of them strong students, were given a hard verbal test. Half were told it was a measure of verbal ability. The other half were told it was a problem-solving exercise — same test, just framed differently. The students who were told it measured verbal ability scored significantly worse. Why? Because for that group, the test now activated a stereotype about their race and intelligence. The fear of confirming the stereotype — the cognitive load of fighting it — depressed performance. They later replicated this with women on math tests. Same effect. The test wasn't the problem. The framing was. Stereotype threat is one of the most replicated findings in modern psychology. It's also one reason 'just measure intelligence with one number' is harder than it sounds.

Recap. Six things. One — three test-quality criteria: reliability, validity, standardization. All three or it isn't really a test. Two — reliability has three types: test-retest, split-half, inter-rater. Validity has three: content, construct, criterion / predictive. Three — Spearman's g says one general factor. Gardner says eight intelligences, weakly supported. Sternberg says three: analytical, creative, practical. Four — IQ tests: Stanford-Binet and Wechsler's WAIS / WISC. Mean a hundred, SD fifteen, bell curve, sixty-eight percent within one SD. Five — the Flynn effect: average IQ has risen about three points per decade. Six — bias matters. Cultural bias in test content; stereotype threat in test framing — Steele and Aronson showed it can change the score itself.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Twelve, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, Module Twelve, on testing and intelligence. Pay extra attention to the difference between reliability and validity, and the bell curve numbers. Two — go to AP Classroom and try fifteen multiple-choice questions on identifying reliability versus validity from short test descriptions. This is the most-tested concept in this module. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: read ten short test scenarios and label each one as reliable, valid, both, or neither. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if you're still mixing up reliability and validity, or if Gardner versus Sternberg is fuzzy. See you in Module Thirteen — abnormal psychology, where we figure out where 'normal' ends and 'disorder' begins, and why that line is harder to draw than you think.
